seo.description: Burst pipe? Sewer backup? Gas leak? Licensed emergency plumbers in Chicago respond fast, 24/7. Call 833-758-6911 now.

emergency-plumber.content:
Emergency Plumbing — When Minutes Matter

A burst pipe at 2 a.m. doesn't wait for business hours. Neither do we. Plumbers 911 connects you with licensed emergency plumbers across Chicagoland who respond fast — day or night, weekends and holidays included.

Whether it's a flooded basement, a sewer backup pushing waste into your home, or a gas line you can smell, we dispatch experienced plumbers who show up with the tools and parts to handle it on the spot.

What Counts as a Plumbing Emergency?

  • Burst or broken pipes — especially in winter when Chicago temps drop below zero
  • Sewer backups — sewage coming up through floor drains or toilets
  • Gas leaks — if you smell gas, leave the house and call immediately
  • No water — complete loss of water pressure or supply
  • Flooding — from a failed sump pump, broken supply line, or water heater rupture
  • Frozen pipes — before they burst, a plumber can thaw them safely

What We Do on an Emergency Call

When you call, we connect you with the nearest available plumber. They'll walk you through immediate steps — like shutting off your main water valve — while en route. On arrival, the plumber assesses the situation, stops the damage, and makes the repair. If a full repair requires follow-up work, they'll stabilize the issue that night and schedule the rest.

Typical Emergency Plumbing Costs

Emergency service rates are higher than scheduled work due to after-hours response and urgency. Here's what to expect in the Chicago area:

  • Emergency service call: $150–$350 (includes dispatch and first hour of labor)
  • Burst pipe repair: $200–$600 depending on location and pipe material
  • Sewer backup clearing: $300–$700 depending on blockage severity
  • Water heater emergency shutoff and repair: $200–$500
  • Gas line emergency repair: $300–$800+

Costs vary by time of day, complexity, and materials needed. Your plumber will explain the scope and cost before starting non-emergency follow-up work.

Frequently Asked Questions

How fast can an emergency plumber get to my Chicago home?

Most emergency calls are responded to within 30–60 minutes, depending on your location and current demand. We cover all of Chicago and the surrounding suburbs.

Do emergency plumbers charge more on weekends and holidays?

After-hours, weekend, and holiday rates are typically higher than standard business-hour rates. Expect a premium of $50–$150 over normal service call fees. Your plumber will discuss pricing before beginning work.

What should I do while waiting for the plumber?

Shut off the water at the main valve if there's active flooding. If you smell gas, leave the house immediately and call from outside. Move valuables away from standing water. Don't attempt electrical work near water.

Can an emergency plumber fix a frozen pipe before it bursts?

Yes. Plumbers use controlled heat methods to safely thaw frozen pipes. If you notice reduced water pressure in winter or frost on exposed pipes, call before it becomes a burst — the repair is much cheaper.

Are your emergency plumbers licensed and insured?

Every plumber in our network is licensed by the State of Illinois and carries full liability insurance. We verify credentials before adding anyone to our referral network.

seo.title: Residential Plumbing Services in Chicago, IL

residential-plumbing.content:
Residential Plumbing for Chicago Homes

From a dripping faucet in a Lincoln Park condo to a full repipe of a 1920s bungalow in Berwyn, we handle every residential plumbing job in Chicagoland. Our licensed plumbers know the housing stock — Greystones, two-flats, ranch homes, new construction — and the unique plumbing challenges each one brings.

What We Cover

  • Repairs: Leaky faucets, running toilets, dripping pipes, slow drains, water pressure problems
  • Installations: Fixtures, water heaters, garbage disposals, dishwashers, water filtration systems
  • Drain & sewer: Cleaning, camera inspections, line replacement
  • Remodeling: Kitchen and bathroom rough-in, fixture placement, supply and drain relocations
  • Maintenance: Seasonal checkups, water heater flushes, sump pump testing, backflow prevention

Common Residential Plumbing Issues in Chicago

Chicago's older housing stock means we see a lot of the same problems: galvanized steel pipes corroding from the inside out, cast iron sewer lines cracked by tree roots, and lead service lines that need replacement. Newer homes aren't immune either — builder-grade fixtures fail, and settling foundations can stress drain lines.

We also see seasonal patterns. Winter brings frozen pipes and water heater failures. Spring means sump pump overloads. Summer is remodeling season. We're busy year-round because Chicago homes keep us busy year-round.

Typical Residential Plumbing Costs

  • Service call + minor repair: $150–$300 (faucet fix, toilet repair, small leak)
  • Fixture installation: $200–$500 per fixture (faucet, toilet, garbage disposal)
  • Water heater replacement: $800–$2,000 (tank) or $2,500–$5,000 (tankless)
  • Drain cleaning: $150–$400 depending on method and access
  • Whole-house repipe: $4,000–$15,000+ depending on home size and pipe material

Every job is different. Your plumber will assess the situation and discuss costs before starting work.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do you work on older Chicago homes with galvanized or lead pipes?

Absolutely. That's a big part of what we do. We regularly repipe homes built before 1960 that still have galvanized supply lines or lead service connections. We'll recommend the best replacement material — typically copper or PEX — based on your home's layout and your budget.

Can you handle both the plumbing and permit for a bathroom remodel?

Yes. Our plumbers pull permits when required by the City of Chicago or suburban municipalities. We know the local codes and inspection requirements, so you don't have to worry about compliance.

How long does a typical residential plumbing repair take?

Most repairs — leaky faucet, running toilet, small leak — take 1–2 hours. Larger jobs like water heater replacements take half a day. Repiping or sewer line work may take 1–3 days depending on scope.

Do you offer plumbing inspections for home buyers?

Yes. We do pre-purchase plumbing inspections that go deeper than a standard home inspection. We check supply lines, drain condition, water heater age, sewer line status via camera inspection, and water pressure.

What areas do you serve beyond Chicago city limits?

We cover all of Cook County and the surrounding collar counties — DuPage, Will, Lake, Kane, McHenry, Kendall, DeKalb, and Grundy. If you're in the Chicagoland area, we've got you covered.

commercial-plumbing.content:
Commercial Plumbing for Chicago Businesses

Commercial plumbing is a different animal than residential. Higher volumes, stricter codes, grease traps, backflow preventers, and systems that can't go down without costing you money every hour. We work with restaurants, office buildings, retail spaces, multi-unit residential properties, and industrial facilities across Chicagoland.

Commercial Services We Provide

  • Drain and sewer maintenance: Scheduled cleaning, grease trap service, camera inspections
  • Backflow prevention: Testing, installation, and certification per Illinois code
  • Water heater systems: Commercial tank and tankless units, boiler connections
  • Fixture installation: ADA-compliant restrooms, commercial kitchen plumbing, bar sinks
  • Gas line work: Restaurant equipment hookups, gas line repairs and extensions
  • Emergency service: 24/7 response for flooding, sewer backups, and pipe failures
  • Tenant buildouts: Plumbing rough-in for new commercial spaces

Typical Commercial Plumbing Costs

  • Commercial service call: $200–$400 (includes diagnostic and first hour)
  • Grease trap cleaning: $200–$500 depending on trap size
  • Backflow preventer testing: $75–$200 per device
  • Commercial water heater replacement: $2,000–$8,000+ depending on capacity
  • Tenant buildout plumbing: $3,000–$15,000+ depending on scope

Commercial jobs are quoted individually based on scope, access, and code requirements. We provide detailed written quotes for larger projects.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do you handle Chicago commercial plumbing permits?

Yes. We pull all required permits and schedule inspections with the City of Chicago or the relevant municipality. Our plumbers know commercial codes and make sure the work passes inspection the first time.

Can you work around our business hours to minimize disruption?

Absolutely. We schedule commercial work for evenings, weekends, or off-peak hours when that makes sense for your operation. For restaurants, we typically work between service hours or overnight.

Do you provide maintenance contracts for commercial properties?

Yes. We work with property managers and business owners on scheduled maintenance plans — regular drain cleaning, backflow testing, water heater inspections, and seasonal prep work.

How quickly can you respond to a commercial plumbing emergency?

We offer 24/7 emergency response for commercial accounts. Most emergency calls are dispatched within the hour. If you have a sewer backup in a restaurant or flooding in an occupied building, call immediately.

Are your plumbers licensed for commercial work in Chicago?

Every plumber in our network holds the appropriate Illinois state license for commercial plumbing work. We also carry full liability and workers' compensation insurance.

seo.title: Bathroom Remodeling Plumbing in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Expert bathroom remodeling plumbing in Chicago — fixture installs, supply lines, drains, shower valves, ADA upgrades. Call 833-758-6911.

bathroom-remodeling.content:
Bathroom Remodeling Plumbing Done Right

A bathroom remodel isn't just tile and vanities — the plumbing behind the walls is what makes it all work. We handle the rough-in, supply lines, drain relocations, fixture installations, and everything in between. Whether you're updating a half-bath or gutting a master suite, we make sure the plumbing is solid before the finish work goes in.

What We Handle

  • Fixture installation: Toilets, sinks, faucets, showerheads, tub fillers
  • Shower and tub plumbing: Valve replacement, tub-to-shower conversions, custom shower builds
  • Supply line upgrades: Replacing old galvanized lines with copper or PEX
  • Drain relocations: Moving drains for new layouts
  • ADA-compliant installations: Walk-in tubs, barrier-free showers, grab bars

Chicago-Specific Considerations

Bathroom remodels in Chicago's older homes often reveal surprises — galvanized pipes that crumble when you touch them, cast iron drains that have rusted through, or non-standard framing that requires creative plumbing solutions. In multi-unit buildings, you also need to coordinate with neighbors and the building association. We've done hundreds of bathroom remodels in every type of Chicago housing.

Typical Bathroom Remodeling Plumbing Costs

  • Basic fixture swap (same locations): $1,500–$3,000 for toilet, sink, and shower/tub valve
  • Fixture relocation (moving drains/supply): $3,000–$6,000+
  • Full bathroom rough-in (new bathroom): $5,000–$10,000+ including supply, drain, and vent
  • Tub-to-shower conversion: $1,500–$4,000 plumbing portion
  • Shower valve replacement: $300–$800 including valve and labor

These are plumbing costs only. Tile, cabinetry, and finish work are separate. Costs vary based on access, pipe material, and code requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a permit for a bathroom remodel in Chicago?

If you're moving plumbing — relocating drains, adding a new bathroom, or changing fixture locations — yes. Simple fixture swaps in the same location typically don't require a permit. We pull permits when required.

Can you move my shower drain to a different location?

Yes. On a concrete slab, this involves cutting the slab and rerouting the drain. On a wood-framed floor, the process is simpler. Both are very doable.

How long does the plumbing portion take?

Rough-in work takes 1–2 days. Fixture installation after tile and finish work takes another half day to a full day. We coordinate with your general contractor on timing.

Should I replace old pipes while the walls are open?

If the walls are already open, that's the time to do it. Replacing corroded galvanized lines or deteriorated cast iron while you have access saves you from tearing things apart again later.

Do you work with my contractor, or do I hire you separately?

Either way works. Many GCs bring us in as their plumbing sub. You can also hire us directly. We're flexible and communicate clearly with all parties involved.

seo.title: Toilet Repair & Installation in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Toilet repair, replacement, and installation in Chicago. Running toilets, clogs, new installs — licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

toilet-install.content:
Toilet Repair & Installation

A toilet that won't stop running, won't flush properly, or keeps clogging isn't just annoying — it's wasting water and money. We fix and replace toilets in homes and businesses across Chicagoland. It's one of our most common calls, and it's usually a quick fix.

Common Toilet Problems We Fix

  • Running toilet: Usually a faulty flapper, fill valve, or overflow tube. Quick repair, big water savings.
  • Weak or incomplete flush: Clogged rim jets, low water level, or a partially blocked trap.
  • Constant clogs: Could be the toilet itself or a deeper drain issue.
  • Leaking at the base: Failed wax ring — needs replacement before subfloor damage.
  • Cracked tank or bowl: Can't be repaired. Replacement is the only fix.

Typical Toilet Service Costs

  • Toilet repair (flapper, fill valve, handle): $100–$250 including parts
  • Wax ring replacement: $150–$300 (toilet must be pulled and reset)
  • Toilet replacement (standard): $300–$600 labor or $450–$900 with toilet included
  • Flange repair: $150–$400 depending on damage

If your toilet needs frequent repairs, replacing it with a modern water-efficient model is usually more cost-effective long term.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I know if my toilet needs repair or replacement?

Single issues like a running flapper or loose handle — repair makes sense. Constant clogs, cracks, or multiple failing parts on a 15+ year old toilet — replacement is the better investment.

What kind of toilet should I buy?

A standard two-piece with a 1.28 GPF rating works well for most Chicago homes. Elongated bowls are more comfortable; round bowls save space in tight bathrooms. Pressure-assisted models flush more powerfully.

Can I install a toilet myself?

A basic swap is possible, but an improperly set wax ring causes hidden leaks, and a broken flange means water damage to the ceiling below. Pro install ensures it's sealed, level, and leak-free.

Why does my toilet keep clogging?

Could be a narrow trapway (common in older low-flow models), a partially blocked downstream drain, or sewer line buildup. If multiple fixtures are slow, the issue is in your drain or sewer line.

How long does a toilet installation take?

About 1–2 hours for a straightforward replacement. Flange repair or floor work adds another hour. New installations requiring drain and supply work take half a day.

seo.title: Kitchen Remodeling Plumbing in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Kitchen remodeling plumbing in Chicago — sink installs, dishwasher hookups, gas lines, drain relocations. Call 833-758-6911.

kitchen-remodeling.content:
Kitchen Remodeling Plumbing

The kitchen is the most plumbing-intensive room in your house. Sink, dishwasher, garbage disposal, ice maker line, gas line for the stove, pot filler — every one needs supply lines, drain connections, or gas hookups done right. We handle the plumbing side so your contractor can focus on cabinets and countertops.

Kitchen Plumbing Services

  • Sink installation: Undermount, farmhouse, double-bowl — supply and drain for any style
  • Faucet installation: Standard, pull-down, touchless, pot fillers
  • Dishwasher hookup: Supply line, drain connection, and air gap
  • Garbage disposal: New installation or replacement
  • Gas line work: Hookups for gas ranges, new gas lines for relocated stoves
  • Drain relocations: Moving the sink drain for a new layout
  • Ice maker lines: Refrigerator water supply for ice and filtered water

Typical Kitchen Plumbing Costs

  • Sink + faucet installation (same location): $300–$600 labor
  • Sink relocation (new drain/supply run): $1,500–$4,000+
  • Dishwasher hookup: $150–$350
  • Garbage disposal installation: $200–$450 including unit
  • Gas line for range: $300–$800 new run; $150–$300 hookup to existing
  • Full kitchen rough-in (new layout): $3,000–$8,000+

Costs depend on whether you're keeping the same layout or moving things. Same-location fixture swaps are quick. Relocating a sink to an island adds significant drain and vent work.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can you move my kitchen sink to an island?

Yes, but it's one of the more involved tasks. An island sink requires running supply and drain lines under the floor, plus proper venting. It's very doable — it just needs to be planned and budgeted for.

Do I need a gas line run for my new range?

If switching from electric to gas or relocating your range, you'll need a new gas line. This requires a licensed plumber, pressure test, and permit. We handle all of it.

Should I replace supply lines and shutoff valves during a remodel?

If the walls are open, yes. Old gate valves corrode and fail. We replace them with quarter-turn ball valves and braided stainless supply lines — reliable and code-compliant.

Can you install a pot filler above my stove?

Yes. A pot filler requires a cold water supply run to the wall behind the range. Easiest during a remodel, but doable as a retrofit with minimal wall repair.

How do you coordinate with my general contractor?

We schedule rough-in after demo and framing, before drywall. Then we return for fixture installation after countertops are set. We communicate directly with your GC on timing.

seo.title: Dishwasher Installation in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Professional dishwasher installation in Chicago — supply line, drain hookup, proper connection. Licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

dishwasher-install.content:
Dishwasher Installation

Installing a dishwasher is more than sliding it under the counter. It needs a hot water supply line, a drain connection with air gap to prevent backflow, and an electrical hookup. We install new dishwashers and replace old ones in homes across Chicagoland.

What the Installation Includes

  • Hot water supply connection: Tap into your hot water line with a proper shutoff valve
  • Drain hookup: Connected to sink drain or disposal with required high loop or air gap
  • Leveling and securing: Anchored to countertop and tested for leaks
  • Old unit removal: Disconnect and pull out the old dishwasher
  • Supply line upgrade: Replace corroded valves that don't shut off properly

Typical Installation Costs

  • Standard replacement (existing hookups): $150–$300 labor
  • New installation (no existing hookups): $300–$600 including supply, drain, and valve
  • Supply valve replacement (add-on): $75–$150

These are labor and connection costs — the dishwasher itself is separate.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can you install a dishwasher where there wasn't one before?

Yes. We'll run a hot water supply and drain connection from your kitchen sink area. Straightforward as long as there's cabinet space within reasonable distance of the sink.

Do I need an air gap for my dishwasher?

Illinois code requires either an air gap or a high drain loop to prevent backflow. We install whichever is appropriate for your setup.

Should I repair or replace a leaking dishwasher?

If it's over 8–10 years old and the leak is from the tub, pump, or door seal, replacement usually makes more sense. A loose connection at supply or drain is a quick fix.

How long does installation take?

Standard replacement: 1–1.5 hours. New installation with supply and drain runs: 2–3 hours. We test for leaks and run a short cycle before leaving.

Can you install any brand of dishwasher?

Yes. Standard dishwashers are 24 inches wide with the same connection types. We install Bosch, Samsung, LG, KitchenAid, Whirlpool, GE, and every other major brand.

seo.title: Faucet Repair & Installation in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Faucet repair and installation in Chicago — dripping faucets, new installs, kitchen and bath. Licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

faucet-repair.content:
Faucet Repair & Installation

A dripping faucet wastes over 3,000 gallons per year. We repair and replace kitchen, bathroom, and utility faucets throughout Chicagoland. Most faucet jobs take an hour or less.

Common Faucet Problems

  • Dripping faucet: Worn cartridge, O-ring, or valve seat. Quick repair.
  • Low water pressure: Clogged aerator, corroded supply lines, or failing cartridge.
  • Handle won't turn: Mineral buildup or seized cartridge — common with Chicago's hard water.
  • Leaking under the sink: Failed supply connections or corroded shutoff valves.
  • Sprayer not working: Diverter valve issue or clogged sprayer head.

Typical Faucet Service Costs

  • Faucet repair (cartridge, O-ring): $100–$250 including parts
  • Faucet replacement (labor only): $150–$300
  • Faucet replacement (with standard faucet): $250–$500
  • Shutoff valve replacement: $75–$200 per valve

If your faucet is more than 10 years old and needs repair, a new faucet may make more sense. Modern faucets are more water-efficient and easier to maintain.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is it worth repairing my old faucet or should I replace it?

If parts are available and the body is in good shape, repair is cheaper. If the faucet is corroded, the finish is worn, or it's builder-grade, replacement gives better long-term value.

Can I use any faucet with my sink?

Match the faucet to the number of holes in your sink or countertop. A single-hole faucet won't cover a three-hole sink without a deck plate. We can advise before you buy.

Why is my new faucet's water pressure low?

Check the aerator — they sometimes have a flow restrictor or installation debris. Also make sure supply valves are fully open. If pressure is still low, the issue may be in your supply lines.

Do you install touchless faucets?

Yes. Touchless faucets need a power source — usually batteries or an AC adapter. We handle all plumbing connections and make sure the sensor works properly.

How long does faucet installation take?

A straightforward swap takes 30–60 minutes. Replacing shutoff valves or supply lines adds another 30 minutes. Removing a stubborn old faucet with corroded connections can add time.

seo.title: Garbage Disposal Installation & Repair in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Garbage disposal installation and repair in Chicago. Jammed, leaking, or new install — licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

garbage-disposal-installation.content:
Garbage Disposal Installation & Repair

A working garbage disposal makes kitchen cleanup easier. A broken one turns your sink into a swamp. We install new disposals and repair existing ones throughout Chicagoland — from basic 1/3 HP units to heavy-duty 1 HP models for family kitchens.

Common Disposal Problems

  • Jammed: Utensil, bone, or hard object stuck in the grinding chamber.
  • Won't turn on: Tripped reset button, blown fuse, or burned-out motor.
  • Leaking: From sink flange (top), discharge pipe (side), or bottom (internal seal failure).
  • Slow draining: Partial clog in the disposal or drain line downstream.
  • Loud grinding: Loose impellers, worn bearings, or debris in the chamber.

Typical Garbage Disposal Costs

  • Repair (jam, reset, drain clearing): $100–$200
  • Replacement (standard 1/2 HP): $200–$400 including unit and labor
  • Replacement (premium 3/4–1 HP): $350–$600 including unit and labor
  • New installation (no existing disposal): $250–$500 including unit, wiring, and drain

If your disposal is humming but not spinning, or leaking from the bottom, replacement is usually more cost-effective than repair.

Frequently Asked Questions

What size garbage disposal do I need?

1–2 people: 1/3 to 1/2 HP. Families of 3–4: 1/2 to 3/4 HP. Heavy cooks or large families: 3/4 to 1 HP. Bigger motors grind faster and jam less.

Can I put a disposal in my condo?

Check your building's rules — some older Chicago condos prohibit disposals due to aging drain lines. If allowed, we can install one.

Why does my disposal smell bad?

Food residue on the splash guard and grinding chamber. Run cold water and ice cubes through to clean. Citrus peels help with odor. Persistent smells may indicate a drain line clog below.

How long do garbage disposals last?

8–12 years with normal use. If yours is approaching that age and starting to jam, leak, or grind slowly, replacement beats repair.

Can you connect my disposal to my dishwasher drain?

Yes. Most dishwashers drain through the disposal. We remove the knockout plug and connect the dishwasher drain hose securely.

seo.title: Sewer Cleaning Services in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Professional sewer cleaning in Chicago — hydro jetting, cable machines, root and grease removal. Licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

sewer-cleaning.content:
Sewer Cleaning

When your main sewer line backs up, everything stops — toilets, showers, sinks. Raw sewage comes back up through the lowest drain. We clear sewer blockages with professional-grade cable machines and hydro jetters, and camera-inspect the line afterward to confirm it's clear and intact.

What Causes Sewer Blockages?

  • Tree roots: The #1 cause in Chicago. Roots infiltrate clay and cast iron joints and grow until they block the line.
  • Grease buildup: Kitchen grease coats the pipe over years, gradually narrowing it.
  • Paper products and wipes: "Flushable" wipes don't break down — they snag on roots and grease.
  • Pipe deterioration: Older clay and cast iron lines develop bellies, cracks, and offsets that trap debris.
  • Heavy rain: Chicago's combined sewer system means storm surges overwhelm old sewer lines.

Our Cleaning Methods

  • Cable machine (snake): Motorized steel cable with cutting head that breaks through roots and debris.
  • Hydro jetting: High-pressure water (3,000–4,000 PSI) that scours pipe walls clean. Best for grease and thorough cleaning.
  • Camera inspection: Post-cleaning camera run to confirm the line is clear and check pipe condition.

Typical Sewer Cleaning Costs

  • Cable/snake (standard blockage): $200–$500
  • Hydro jetting: $400–$900
  • Camera inspection (standalone): $150–$400
  • Emergency sewer backup (after-hours): $350–$800

If root intrusion is the cause, you may need periodic cleaning until the line is replaced. We'll tell you what we find and what your options are.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I know if it's my sewer line or just a drain clog?

One slow fixture = localized drain clog. Multiple slow fixtures, especially on the lowest level = main sewer line. Sewage through a basement floor drain is the clearest sign.

How often should I have my sewer line cleaned?

If you've had backups: every 1–2 years. Large trees near the line: annual cleaning. Newer PVC lines with no tree issues may never need it.

Will sewer cleaning damage my pipes?

Professional equipment is designed to clean without damage. If pipes are severely deteriorated, cleaning may reveal underlying problems — better to find them during maintenance than during a backup.

What's the difference between cable and hydro jetting?

Cable punches through the blockage. Hydro jetting cleans the entire pipe wall and removes grease and scale. Jetting is more thorough and lasts longer — like pressure-washing the inside of the pipe.

Do you clean sewer lines in winter?

Yes, year-round. Sewer backups don't take the winter off, and neither do we. Our crews are equipped for Chicago winters.

seo.title: Sewer Line Replacement in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Sewer line replacement in Chicago — trenchless and traditional methods for cracked, collapsed, or root-damaged lines. Call 833-758-6911.

sewer-replacement.content:
Sewer Line Replacement

When cleaning can't fix it — because the pipe is cracked, collapsed, bellied, or disintegrating — replacement is the answer. We replace sewer lines using both traditional excavation and trenchless methods, depending on what makes sense for your property.

Signs You Need Replacement

  • Recurring backups even after professional cleaning
  • Camera inspection showing cracks, offsets, bellies, or pervasive root infiltration
  • Sewage odor in your yard or basement
  • Sinkholes or wet spots along the sewer line path
  • Old pipe material: Orangeburg, clay, or severely corroded cast iron

Replacement Methods

  • Traditional excavation: Trench along the line, remove old pipe, lay new PVC. Best for collapsed lines.
  • Pipe bursting (trenchless): New HDPE pipe pulled through old one. Minimal digging — usually just two access pits.
  • Pipe lining (CIPP): Resin-coated liner cured inside existing pipe. Good for cracks and joint issues without major collapse.

Typical Sewer Replacement Costs

  • Traditional excavation: $5,000–$15,000
  • Trenchless pipe bursting: $6,000–$18,000
  • CIPP lining: $4,000–$12,000
  • Landscape/concrete restoration: $500–$3,000+ (may be included)

Permits and inspections are typically required. We handle the paperwork. Costs vary by pipe length, depth, access, and obstacles.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does sewer replacement take?

Traditional excavation: 1–3 days. Trenchless methods: often completed in a single day. CIPP lining: 1–2 days including cure time.

Is trenchless always better?

Not always. Trenchless works when the existing pipe is relatively straight and hasn't fully collapsed. Severe bellies, offsets, or total collapse may require traditional excavation. A camera inspection tells us which method works.

How long will the new sewer line last?

PVC and HDPE sewer pipes have a 50–100+ year lifespan. They don't corrode, resist roots better than clay or cast iron, and handle Chicago's freeze-thaw cycles well.

Do I need to replace the whole line or just part?

Depends on the camera findings. Isolated damage = spot repair. Entire line deteriorated = full replacement prevents future problems and repeated repairs.

Will sewer replacement tear up my yard?

Traditional excavation requires trenching. We restore the trench and replace sod. Trenchless methods minimize this — typically just two small pits at each end.

seo.title: Sewer Camera Inspection in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Sewer camera inspection in Chicago — see inside your sewer line before you dig. Root intrusion, cracks, blockages identified. Call 833-758-6911.

sewer-camera-inspection.content:
Sewer Camera Inspection

A sewer camera inspection lets us see exactly what's happening inside your sewer line without digging. We push a waterproof camera through the pipe on a flexible cable and watch the live feed. It shows roots, cracks, offsets, grease buildup, bellies, and blockages — and tells us exactly where the problem is.

When to Get a Camera Inspection

  • Recurring drain or sewer backups — to find the root cause
  • Buying a home — especially in Chicago where sewer lines are often 50+ years old
  • After sewer cleaning — to confirm the line is clear and check condition
  • Before a remodel — to assess the line before adding fixtures
  • Sewage odor that can't be explained by a simple clog

What the Camera Reveals

  • Root intrusion: Roots growing through pipe joints — very common in clay lines
  • Cracks and fractures: From settling, frost heave, or age
  • Bellies (sags): Low spots where water and debris collect
  • Offsets: Pipe sections shifted apart at joints
  • Pipe material and condition: Clay, cast iron, Orangeburg, PVC — we identify what you have

Typical Camera Inspection Costs

  • Standard inspection: $150–$400
  • With sewer cleaning (bundled): $350–$700 combined
  • Locator service (marking pipe path): $100–$200 add-on

A camera inspection is one of the best investments before buying a home or deciding on repair vs. replacement.

Frequently Asked Questions

How does a sewer camera inspection work?

We access your sewer line through a cleanout. The camera head is pushed through the line on a flexible cable. Video shows the pipe interior in real time, and a location transmitter marks problems from above ground.

Should I get one before buying a house?

In Chicago, absolutely. Standard home inspections don't include the sewer line. A $200–$400 camera inspection can save you from a $10,000+ surprise replacement after closing.

How long does an inspection take?

30–60 minutes. We show you the footage in real time and explain what we're seeing. You get a summary and our recommendation for next steps.

Can the camera reach the city main?

In most cases, yes. Our cameras travel 100+ feet. If a blockage prevents passage, we note the location and nature of the obstruction.

Do you provide a recording?

Yes. We provide video or photos for your records — especially useful for real estate transactions or insurance claims.

seo.title: Sewer Cleanout Installation in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Sewer cleanout installation in Chicago — easy access for future sewer maintenance and inspections. Licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

sewer-cleanout-installation.content:
Sewer Cleanout Installation

A sewer cleanout is a capped access point that lets a plumber quickly access the line for cleaning and camera inspections. Many older Chicago homes don't have one, which means every sewer backup requires pulling a toilet or digging. Installing a cleanout saves time and money on every future service call.

Why You Need a Cleanout

  • Faster service: Plumbers access the line in minutes instead of removing fixtures
  • Lower costs: No extra labor to pull and reset a toilet each time
  • Camera inspections: Ideal entry point for sewer cameras
  • Code compliance: Required for new construction and major sewer work
  • Real estate value: Buyers and inspectors look for accessible cleanouts

Types of Cleanouts

  • Interior: In the basement where the sewer exits the foundation. Year-round access.
  • Exterior: In the yard near the property line. Access to main run.
  • Two-way: Access in both directions — toward house and toward street.

Typical Cleanout Installation Costs

  • Interior (basement): $400–$1,000
  • Exterior (yard): $800–$2,000
  • Two-way cleanout: $1,000–$2,500

The cost pays for itself quickly with regular sewer maintenance. Without a cleanout, each cleaning call costs extra for toilet removal.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I know if my house has a cleanout?

Look for a capped pipe (usually 4 inches) in your yard near the foundation, or in your basement near the main drain exit. If you can't find one, you probably don't have one — common in older Chicago homes.

Does installation require digging?

Exterior cleanouts require excavation. Interior cleanouts involve cutting into the drain pipe — no digging, but concrete may need to be cut if under the slab.

Is a cleanout required by code?

For new construction and significant sewer work, yes. Existing homes without one aren't retroactively required to add one, but it's one of the smartest plumbing upgrades you can make.

Can you add a cleanout during other sewer work?

Absolutely — and that's the most cost-effective time. If we're already accessing or replacing your sewer line, adding a cleanout is significantly cheaper as an add-on.

How long does installation take?

Interior: 2–4 hours. Exterior: half to full day depending on depth and soil. We restore excavated areas to grade when done.

seo.title: Drain Cleaning Services in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Professional drain cleaning in Chicago — kitchen, bathroom, floor drains, main lines. Licensed plumbers with proper equipment. Call 833-758-6911.

drain-cleaning.content:
Drain Cleaning

A slow drain is a clogged drain that hasn't finished clogging yet. Whether it's the kitchen sink, bathroom shower, or basement floor drain, we clear it with the right tool — not a bottle of chemical drain cleaner that eats your pipes from the inside out.

Common Drain Problems

  • Kitchen drains: Grease, food particles, and soap buildup that narrows the pipe over time.
  • Bathroom drains: Hair, soap scum, and toothpaste. Shower and tub drains are the worst offenders.
  • Floor drains: Sediment, debris, and dried-out traps. Can also back up from the sewer.
  • Laundry drains: Lint and detergent residue clog more than people expect.
  • Multiple slow drains: Problem is downstream — possibly your main sewer line.

How We Clean Drains

  • Hand snake: Flexible cable for sink and tub clogs within 25 feet of the fixture.
  • Motorized drain machine: Powered cable for tougher blockages in larger lines.
  • Hydro jetting: High-pressure water that scours the pipe clean. Best for grease and recurring problems.

Typical Drain Cleaning Costs

  • Single drain (sink, tub, shower): $100–$250
  • Main drain line: $200–$400
  • Hydro jetting: $400–$900
  • Floor drain cleaning: $150–$300

Chemical drain cleaners corrode pipes — especially the older cast iron and galvanized lines common in Chicago homes. Professional cleaning is safer and more effective.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are chemical drain cleaners safe?

We don't recommend them. Caustic chemicals corrode pipes, especially older metal pipes. They also don't work on complete blockages and can make a plumber's job harder if sitting in the drain when we arrive.

Why does my drain keep clogging?

Recurring clogs usually mean buildup that a quick snake isn't fully removing, or a structural pipe issue — belly, offset, or root intrusion. A camera inspection shows what's going on.

How often should drains be professionally cleaned?

Most drains don't need regular cleaning with proper use. Kitchen drains benefit from professional cleaning every 1–2 years if you cook frequently. Sewer lines with root issues may need annual maintenance.

Can I prevent drain clogs?

Use drain strainers. Don't pour grease down the drain — wipe greasy pans with a paper towel first. Run hot water after dishes. Never flush anything besides toilet paper.

What if my floor drain smells bad?

The P-trap holds water to block sewer gas. If the drain isn't used regularly, water evaporates. Pour a bucket of water in to refill the trap. If the smell persists, there may be a blockage or broken trap.

seo.title: Drain Line Replacement in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Drain line replacement in Chicago — corroded, cracked, or collapsed drain pipes replaced with modern materials. Call 833-758-6911.

drain-replacement.content:
Drain Line Replacement

When a drain line is cracked, corroded, collapsed, or has repeated blockages that cleaning can't solve, replacement is the permanent fix. We replace interior and exterior drain lines using modern PVC and ABS materials that outlast the originals by decades.

Signs Your Drain Line Needs Replacement

  • Recurring clogs in the same line despite professional cleaning
  • Slow drainage that doesn't improve after snaking
  • Sewage odor from the drain even when it's not clogged
  • Visible corrosion on exposed cast iron or galvanized drain pipes
  • Water damage on ceilings or walls below drain lines
  • Camera inspection showing cracks, bellies, or collapse

What We Replace

  • Kitchen and bathroom drain lines: From fixture to main stack
  • Main drain stack: The vertical pipe connecting upper floors to the sewer
  • Underground drains: Below-slab drain lines in basements
  • Laundry and utility drains: Corroded galvanized or cast iron lines

Typical Drain Replacement Costs

  • Single drain line (fixture to stack): $500–$2,000
  • Main drain stack replacement: $2,000–$6,000
  • Below-slab drain replacement: $2,500–$8,000+ (includes concrete cutting and restoration)
  • Whole-house drain replacement: $5,000–$15,000+

Costs vary based on pipe material, access, length, and whether concrete or finished walls need to be opened. We restore all access points when the work is complete.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I know if my drains need replacement vs. just cleaning?

If professional cleaning solves the problem and it stays solved for a year or more, cleaning is enough. If the same line clogs every few months, or a camera shows structural damage, replacement is the long-term answer.

What material do you use for replacement drains?

PVC for most residential drain replacements. It's durable, smooth (resists buildup), lightweight, and code-compliant. For below-slab work, we use Schedule 40 PVC. Cast iron is sometimes used in multi-unit buildings for noise reduction.

Do you need to cut into my walls or floor?

It depends on where the damaged pipe is. Exposed basement pipes are straightforward. Pipes inside walls or under concrete slabs require opening access. We minimize the opening and restore it after the work.

How long does drain replacement take?

A single drain line: half a day to a full day. Main stack replacement: 1–2 days. Below-slab work: 1–3 days including concrete cutting and restoration.

Can you replace just the damaged section?

Yes. If the damage is isolated, a spot repair saves money. If the entire line is deteriorated, full replacement prevents future failures and repeated repairs.

seo.title: Water Heater Repair in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Water heater repair in Chicago — no hot water, leaking tanks, pilot light issues, and more. Licensed plumbers, 24/7. Call 833-758-6911.

water-heater-repair.content:
Water Heater Repair

No hot water in a Chicago winter is an emergency. We diagnose and repair gas and electric water heaters — tank and tankless — throughout Chicagoland. Most repairs can be done the same day, and we carry the common parts on our trucks.

Common Water Heater Problems

  • No hot water: Dead pilot light, failed thermocouple, bad heating element, or tripped reset
  • Not enough hot water: Sediment buildup, undersized tank, or a failing dip tube
  • Water too hot: Thermostat set too high or a malfunctioning thermostat
  • Leaking tank: Could be a relief valve, a fitting, or the tank itself (tank corrosion = replacement)
  • Strange noises: Popping or rumbling usually means sediment buildup on the bottom of the tank
  • Rusty or smelly water: Corroded anode rod or bacteria in the tank

Typical Water Heater Repair Costs

  • Thermocouple replacement: $100–$200
  • Heating element replacement (electric): $150–$300
  • Thermostat replacement: $150–$300
  • T&P relief valve replacement: $100–$250
  • Anode rod replacement: $150–$300
  • Dip tube replacement: $150–$300
  • Tank flush (sediment removal): $100–$200

If your water heater is over 10 years old and the tank is leaking, replacement is usually more practical than repair. We'll give you an honest assessment.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long do water heaters last?

Tank water heaters typically last 8–12 years. Tankless units can last 15–20 years with proper maintenance. Hard water (common in Chicago suburbs) shortens lifespan due to mineral buildup.

Why is my water heater making popping sounds?

Sediment has settled on the bottom of the tank. When the burner heats the water, it creates steam bubbles under the sediment layer. A tank flush can solve this. If it's been years since a flush, the sediment may be hardite and the tank may need replacement.

Can a water heater be repaired if it's leaking from the bottom?

If the leak is from the drain valve or a fitting, yes. If the tank itself is leaking (internal corrosion), no — the tank can't be patched. Replacement is the only option.

Should I repair or replace my water heater?

If the unit is under 8 years old and the repair is under $400, repair makes sense. If it's over 10, or the tank is corroding, invest in a new one. You'll get better efficiency and a fresh warranty.

Do you repair both gas and electric water heaters?

Yes. We work on all major brands — Rheem, AO Smith, Bradford White, State, and others. Gas and electric, tank and tankless.

seo.title: Water Heater Installation in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Water heater installation in Chicago — tank and tankless units, gas and electric. Licensed plumbers with same-day service. Call 833-758-6911.

water-heater-installation.content:
Water Heater Installation

Whether your old water heater just died or you're upgrading to a more efficient unit, we handle the full installation — removal of the old unit, setting the new one, connecting gas or electric, plumbing the water lines, and making sure everything is up to code.

Types of Water Heaters We Install

  • Standard tank (gas): The most common type in Chicago homes. 40–50 gallon tanks for most families.
  • Standard tank (electric): Good option where gas isn't available. Lower upfront cost, higher operating cost.
  • Tankless (gas): Endless hot water, smaller footprint, higher efficiency. Higher upfront cost.
  • Tankless (electric): Point-of-use units for single fixtures or small whole-house units.
  • High-efficiency condensing: 90%+ efficiency, vents through PVC instead of metal flue.

What Installation Includes

  • Disconnection and removal of old water heater
  • New water heater set in place and leveled
  • Gas line or electrical connection
  • Water supply and discharge piping
  • T&P relief valve and discharge pipe
  • Expansion tank (if required by code)
  • Venting (gas units)
  • Testing and temperature adjustment

Typical Water Heater Installation Costs

  • Standard 40–50 gal tank (gas): $800–$1,800 including unit and labor
  • Standard 40–50 gal tank (electric): $700–$1,500 including unit and labor
  • Tankless (gas): $2,500–$5,000 including unit, venting, and gas line upgrades
  • High-efficiency condensing: $1,500–$3,000 including unit and labor
  • Old unit disposal: Usually included in the installation price

Tankless installations cost more upfront but save 20–30% on water heating energy costs. We help you weigh the investment based on your household size and hot water usage.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does water heater installation take?

A standard tank swap takes 2–4 hours. Tankless installations take 4–8 hours due to additional venting and gas line work. If we're converting from tank to tankless, it may require a full day.

What size water heater do I need?

For a family of 2–3: a 40-gallon tank or a tankless unit rated for 2–3 simultaneous fixtures. Family of 4–5: a 50-gallon tank or a larger tankless unit. We'll size it based on your household and usage patterns.

Do I need a permit for water heater installation in Chicago?

The City of Chicago requires a permit for water heater replacement. We pull the permit and schedule the inspection as part of the job. Suburban requirements vary — we know the codes for each municipality.

Should I switch from tank to tankless?

Tankless is worth considering if you want endless hot water, have limited space, or plan to stay in the home 10+ years to recoup the higher upfront cost. For homes with high simultaneous demand, a large tank or multiple tankless units may be needed.

Can I install a water heater myself?

We don't recommend it. Gas water heaters involve gas connections, venting, and combustion safety. Electric units involve high-amperage wiring. Both require code-compliant installation. Improper installation can void the warranty and create safety hazards.

seo.title: Tankless Water Heater Repair in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Tankless water heater repair in Chicago — error codes, no hot water, fluctuating temps. Licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

tankless-water-heater-repair.content:
Tankless Water Heater Repair

Tankless water heaters are reliable, but they're not maintenance-free. When they throw an error code, produce inconsistent temperatures, or stop heating altogether, you need a plumber who understands how these units work. We repair all major tankless brands throughout Chicagoland.

Common Tankless Problems

  • Error codes: Each brand has specific codes — ignition failure, flame loss, flow sensor issues, exhaust blockage
  • No hot water: Could be ignition failure, gas supply issue, or frozen condensate line (winter)
  • Fluctuating temperatures: Often a flow rate issue, dirty heat exchanger, or crossover in the plumbing
  • Mineral scale buildup: Chicago's hard water deposits calcium on the heat exchanger, reducing efficiency
  • Low flow rate: Clogged inlet filter, scaled heat exchanger, or undersized unit for demand

Maintenance Matters

Tankless water heaters should be flushed with descaling solution annually — more often in areas with hard water. This removes mineral scale from the heat exchanger and keeps the unit running efficiently. Skipping maintenance is the #1 cause of premature tankless failure.

Typical Tankless Repair Costs

  • Diagnostic + minor repair: $150–$350
  • Descaling/flush service: $150–$300
  • Ignition or flame rod replacement: $200–$400
  • Flow sensor replacement: $200–$400
  • Heat exchanger replacement: $500–$1,500 (often worth considering a new unit)

If the heat exchanger fails outside of warranty, a new unit is often more cost-effective than the repair. We'll give you an honest comparison.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my tankless water heater fluctuate between hot and cold?

This "cold water sandwich" effect happens when the unit cycles on and off during intermittent use. It can also indicate a dirty heat exchanger, crossover from another fixture, or a flow rate below the unit's activation threshold.

How often should a tankless water heater be flushed?

Annually for most homes. In areas with very hard water (common in Chicago suburbs), every 6 months. Regular flushing extends the unit's life and maintains efficiency.

What brands do you service?

We repair Rinnai, Navien, Noritz, Takagi, Rheem, Bosch, and other major brands. We carry common parts and can order brand-specific components quickly.

My tankless shows an error code — what should I do?

Note the error code and call us. Don't try to reset the unit repeatedly — some codes indicate safety issues. We'll diagnose the specific code and make the repair.

Is it worth repairing an old tankless water heater?

Tankless units last 15–20 years. If yours is under 10 and the repair is reasonable, fix it. Over 15 with a major component failure, replacement makes more sense.

seo.title: Tankless Water Heater Installation in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Tankless water heater installation in Chicago — endless hot water, energy savings. Gas and electric units. Call 833-758-6911.

tankless-water-heater-installation.content:
Tankless Water Heater Installation

Tankless water heaters heat water on demand — no stored tank, no running out of hot water, and 20–30% less energy used compared to a standard tank. We install gas and electric tankless units in homes across Chicagoland, including conversions from traditional tank heaters.

Why Go Tankless?

  • Endless hot water: Heats water as it flows — no waiting for a tank to recover
  • Energy savings: Only heats water when you need it. 20–30% more efficient than tank units.
  • Space savings: Wall-mounted units free up floor space
  • Longer lifespan: 15–20 years vs. 8–12 for tank heaters
  • Cleaner water: No standing water in a corroding tank

What Installation Involves

  • Removing old water heater (if converting)
  • Mounting the tankless unit on the wall
  • Upgrading gas line (tankless units often need a larger gas supply)
  • Installing stainless steel venting (gas units)
  • Connecting water supply and discharge lines
  • Electrical connection for controls
  • Condensate drain (condensing models)
  • System testing and temperature programming

Typical Installation Costs

  • Gas tankless (whole-house): $2,500–$5,000 including unit, venting, gas line
  • Electric tankless (whole-house): $1,500–$3,500 including unit and electrical work
  • Electric tankless (point-of-use): $300–$800 per unit
  • Tank-to-tankless conversion: Add $500–$1,500 for gas line upgrade and new venting

The higher upfront cost is offset by lower monthly energy bills and a longer unit lifespan. Most homeowners see payback within 5–7 years.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can my home handle a tankless water heater?

Most homes can. Gas tankless units may need a larger gas line and dedicated venting. Electric units may need a panel upgrade. We assess your home's infrastructure before recommending a unit.

What size tankless unit do I need?

Size is based on flow rate (GPM) and temperature rise. In Chicago, where incoming water is cold (around 45°F in winter), you need more heating capacity. A family of 4 typically needs a unit rated at 8–10 GPM for gas.

Will I really never run out of hot water?

You won't run out, but you can exceed the unit's capacity if too many fixtures run simultaneously. Proper sizing prevents this. For large homes, we may recommend two units or a recirculation system.

Do tankless water heaters work in Chicago winters?

Yes. The unit works harder because incoming water is colder, which slightly reduces flow capacity. We size the unit for Chicago's winter water temperatures, not just summer.

How long does tankless installation take?

A straightforward installation takes 4–6 hours. Conversions from tank to tankless take 6–8 hours due to gas line and venting modifications. We complete most installations in a single day.

seo.title: Backflow Testing & Installation in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Backflow preventer testing and installation in Chicago — annual certification, RPZ and DCV devices. Licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

backflow-testing-installation.content:
Backflow Testing & Installation

Backflow preventers keep contaminated water from flowing backward into the clean water supply. Illinois requires annual testing of these devices, and Chicago's water department enforces it. We install, test, and certify backflow prevention devices for residential and commercial properties.

Why Backflow Prevention Matters

Without a working backflow preventer, a drop in water pressure can pull contaminated water — from irrigation systems, boilers, fire sprinklers, or commercial equipment — back into the potable water supply. It's a serious health hazard and a code violation.

Our Backflow Services

  • Annual testing: Required by Illinois code. We test, certify, and file the results.
  • New installation: RPZ (Reduced Pressure Zone), DCV (Double Check Valve), and PVB (Pressure Vacuum Breaker) devices
  • Repair and rebuild: Replacing internal components when a device fails testing
  • Replacement: When a device is too old or damaged to repair

Typical Backflow Service Costs

  • Annual backflow test (per device): $75–$200
  • Backflow repair/rebuild: $150–$500 depending on device type
  • New RPZ installation: $800–$2,500 depending on size and location
  • New DCV installation: $500–$1,500

Annual testing is an ongoing requirement. We send reminders and can set up a recurring service schedule.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a backflow preventer?

If you have a sprinkler/irrigation system, boiler, fire suppression system, or any connection to non-potable water, yes. Commercial properties almost always require them. Residential requirements vary by municipality.

How often does a backflow preventer need testing?

Annually. Illinois requires certified testing every year, and results must be filed with the local water authority. Failure to test can result in fines or water service disconnection.

What happens if my backflow preventer fails the test?

We repair or rebuild it on the spot if possible. Common issues are worn check valves or relief valve seats. After repair, we re-test and certify. If the device is beyond repair, we replace it.

Who is certified to test backflow preventers?

Only plumbers with a CCCDI (Cross-Connection Control Device Inspector) certification can test and certify backflow devices in Illinois. Our testers hold current certification.

Can I get fined for not testing my backflow preventer?

Yes. The City of Chicago and suburban water departments issue violation notices and fines for non-compliance. In some cases, they can shut off water service until the device is tested and certified.

seo.title: Whole House Repiping in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Whole house repiping in Chicago — replace old galvanized, lead, or polybutylene pipes with copper or PEX. Call 833-758-6911.

whole-house-repiping.content:
Whole House Repiping

If your home still has galvanized steel, lead, or polybutylene water lines, it's time to repipe. Galvanized pipes corrode from the inside, restricting flow and turning water brown. Lead pipes are a health hazard. Polybutylene is prone to sudden failure. We replace old piping throughout your home with modern copper or PEX — restoring full water pressure and clean water to every fixture.

Signs You Need Repiping

  • Low water pressure: Throughout the house, not just one fixture. Corroded pipes restrict flow.
  • Discolored water: Brown, yellow, or rusty water from hot and cold taps.
  • Frequent leaks: Multiple pinhole leaks in different locations — the whole system is failing.
  • Galvanized pipes: Common in Chicago homes built before 1960. Past their 40–60 year lifespan.
  • Lead pipes: Found in some pre-1950s Chicago homes. Should be replaced for health reasons.
  • Visible corrosion: Green, white, or orange deposits on exposed pipes.

Pipe Material Options

  • Copper: Industry standard. Durable, code-compliant everywhere, 50+ year lifespan. Higher material cost.
  • PEX (cross-linked polyethylene): Flexible, freeze-resistant, faster to install. Lower material cost. Accepted by most Chicago-area codes.
  • Copper + PEX hybrid: Copper for exposed runs, PEX for in-wall and in-floor runs. Balances cost and durability.

Typical Whole-House Repiping Costs

  • Small home (1 bath, 1,000 sq ft): $4,000–$7,000
  • Average home (2 bath, 1,500 sq ft): $6,000–$12,000
  • Large home (3+ bath, 2,500+ sq ft): $10,000–$18,000+
  • Drywall/plaster repair (patching access holes): $500–$2,000+

PEX repiping is typically 20–30% less than copper due to lower material cost and faster installation. We provide both options in our quote.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does whole-house repiping take?

Most homes take 2–4 days. Day one involves running new pipes. Day two connects all fixtures. We minimize time without water — typically only a few hours during the cutover.

Do I need to move out during repiping?

No. We work room by room and keep disruption manageable. You'll be without water for a few hours during the main connections, but we plan around your schedule.

Will repiping damage my walls?

We need small access holes in walls and ceilings to run new pipes. We keep them as small as possible and can patch or arrange for a drywall contractor. In homes with accessible basements and attics, wall access is minimal.

Should I choose copper or PEX?

Both are excellent. Copper is proven and lasts 50+ years but costs more. PEX is flexible (fewer fittings, fewer potential leak points), freeze-resistant, and more affordable. In Chicago, most homeowners choose PEX for the savings and performance.

Will repiping improve my water pressure?

If low pressure is caused by corroded pipes (which it usually is in older homes), yes — dramatically. New pipes restore the full flow your fixtures were designed for.

seo.title: Water Softener Installation in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Water softener installation in Chicago — hard water solutions for homes and businesses. Licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

water-softener-installation.content:
Water Softener Installation

Chicago's suburban water is hard — loaded with calcium and magnesium that leaves scale on fixtures, spots on dishes, and buildup inside your water heater and pipes. A water softener removes those minerals before they reach your plumbing. We install and service water softeners for homes across Chicagoland.

Signs You Need a Water Softener

  • White scale buildup on faucets, showerheads, and inside kettles
  • Spots on dishes and glassware after running the dishwasher
  • Dry skin and hair after showering
  • Stiff, faded laundry despite using plenty of detergent
  • Reduced water heater efficiency from scale buildup on heating elements
  • Frequent plumbing repairs due to mineral deposits in valves and fixtures

What Installation Includes

  • Water hardness testing
  • Selecting the right unit size for your household
  • Connecting to the main water supply line
  • Drain line connection for regeneration
  • Bypass valve installation for maintenance
  • Programming and testing

Typical Water Softener Costs

  • Standard ion-exchange softener: $1,200–$3,000 including unit and installation
  • High-capacity or dual-tank: $2,500–$5,000
  • Salt-free conditioner: $1,000–$2,500 (reduces scale but doesn't remove minerals)
  • Ongoing salt costs: $5–$10/month for standard salt bags

A softener extends the life of your water heater, fixtures, and appliances. Most homeowners see savings on soap, detergent, and appliance replacements within a few years.

Frequently Asked Questions

How hard is Chicago's water?

Chicago city water (from Lake Michigan) is moderately hard at about 8 grains per gallon. Suburban well water can be much harder — 15–25+ grains. We test your water before recommending a unit size.

Where does the softener get installed?

Typically near the main water entry point — basement or utility room. It needs access to a drain for regeneration and a power outlet. We also install a bypass valve so you can service the unit without shutting off water to the house.

Do water softeners waste water?

They use 40–60 gallons per regeneration cycle (every few days). Modern high-efficiency units use less. The water and appliance savings more than offset the regeneration water use.

Is softened water safe to drink?

Yes, though it does add a small amount of sodium. If you're on a low-sodium diet, we can install a separate unsoftened line to the kitchen sink or add a reverse osmosis filter for drinking water.

How long do water softeners last?

10–15 years with proper maintenance. That means keeping the salt level up, cleaning the brine tank periodically, and replacing the resin bed when it stops softening effectively.

seo.title: Water Filter Installation & Replacement in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Water filter installation and replacement in Chicago — whole-house, under-sink, and reverse osmosis systems. Call 833-758-6911.

water-filter-installation-replacement.content:
Water Filter Installation & Replacement

Whether you want cleaner drinking water, better-tasting water from every tap, or filtration for a specific contaminant, we install and service water filtration systems for homes across Chicagoland. From simple under-sink filters to whole-house systems, we match the right filter to your water quality concerns.

Types of Water Filters We Install

  • Under-sink filters: Carbon block or sediment filters for a single drinking water tap. Most affordable option.
  • Reverse osmosis (RO): Multi-stage filtration that removes 95%+ of contaminants. Installs under the kitchen sink with a dedicated faucet.
  • Whole-house filtration: Treats all water entering your home — every faucet, shower, and appliance.
  • UV disinfection: Kills bacteria and viruses. Used with well water or as an additional safety layer.
  • Specialty filters: Lead removal, iron/manganese reduction, chloramine reduction

Typical Water Filter Costs

  • Under-sink carbon filter: $150–$400 installed
  • Reverse osmosis system: $400–$1,200 installed
  • Whole-house sediment/carbon filter: $800–$2,500 installed
  • Whole-house specialty filtration: $1,500–$4,000+
  • Filter replacement (ongoing): $30–$150/year depending on system type

We recommend testing your water first to determine what contaminants you're dealing with. That way you invest in filtration that actually addresses your specific water quality issues.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Chicago tap water safe to drink?

Chicago's water meets EPA standards, but it does contain chloramine (disinfectant), some lead risk from older service lines, and other trace contaminants. A quality filter adds an extra layer of protection, especially in older homes.

What's the difference between a water softener and a water filter?

A softener removes hardness minerals (calcium and magnesium). A filter removes contaminants like chlorine, lead, sediment, and bacteria. They address different problems and can be used together.

How often do filters need to be replaced?

Carbon filters: every 6–12 months. RO membranes: every 2–3 years. Sediment pre-filters: every 3–6 months. We can set up a replacement schedule and provide the filters.

Can you install a filter on my refrigerator water line?

Yes. An inline filter on the refrigerator supply line improves ice and water quality. Simple installation — usually under an hour.

Do I need a whole-house filter or just under the sink?

If your concern is drinking water only, an under-sink RO system is the most effective and affordable option. If you want filtered water at every tap and shower (helpful for chloramine removal and skin sensitivity), go whole-house.

frozen-broken-pipe-repair.content:
Frozen & Broken Pipe Repair

Chicago winters are brutal on pipes. When temperatures drop below zero — which happens every year — unprotected water lines freeze, expand, and burst. A single burst pipe can dump hundreds of gallons of water into your home in minutes. We respond to frozen and burst pipe emergencies 24/7 and can have a plumber at your door fast.

Signs of Frozen Pipes

  • No water from a faucet that was working yesterday
  • Reduced flow — a trickle instead of full pressure
  • Frost visible on exposed pipes in the basement, crawl space, or exterior walls
  • Unusual sounds — clanking or banging when you open a tap
  • Bulging or cracked pipe — ice has already expanded inside

What We Do

  • Locate the freeze: We find exactly where the pipe is frozen using thermal detection and pipe knowledge.
  • Thaw safely: Controlled heat application to thaw without causing a burst. Never use open flame.
  • Repair bursts: If the pipe has already burst, we isolate the section, cut out the damage, and splice in new pipe.
  • Prevent future freezing: Pipe insulation, heat tape, and rerouting recommendations for problem areas.

Typical Frozen/Broken Pipe Repair Costs

  • Pipe thawing (no burst): $150–$400
  • Burst pipe repair (accessible): $200–$600
  • Burst pipe repair (in wall or ceiling): $400–$1,200+ including access and patching
  • Emergency after-hours response: Add $100–$200 to above rates
  • Pipe insulation (preventive): $100–$500 depending on length and access

Water damage from a burst pipe is far more expensive than the pipe repair itself. Act fast and shut off the water main as soon as you notice a burst.

Frequently Asked Questions

What should I do if I think my pipes are frozen?

Open the faucet to relieve pressure. If you can see the frozen section, apply gentle heat — a hair dryer or warm towels. Never use a torch or open flame. If you can't find or reach the freeze, call us immediately before it bursts.

Which pipes freeze first?

Pipes in exterior walls, unheated crawl spaces, garages, and uninsulated attics freeze first. In Chicago's older homes, supply lines running through exterior brick walls are especially vulnerable.

How can I prevent frozen pipes?

Insulate exposed pipes, especially in cold areas. Let faucets drip during extreme cold. Keep cabinet doors open under kitchen and bathroom sinks on exterior walls. Maintain heat at 55°F minimum, even if you're away.

Does homeowner's insurance cover burst pipe damage?

Most policies cover sudden water damage from burst pipes, but not the pipe repair itself or damage from gradual leaks. Check your policy and document damage thoroughly with photos.

Can you repair a pipe without opening the wall?

If the burst is behind a wall, we need to open access to reach it. We keep the opening as small as possible. For accessible basement or crawl space pipes, no wall work is needed.

gas-line-install-repair.content:
Gas Line Installation & Repair

Gas line work requires a licensed plumber — no exceptions. Whether you're hooking up a new gas range, running a line to a fireplace, or dealing with a suspected gas leak, we handle it safely and to code. We install and repair gas lines for homes and businesses throughout Chicagoland.

Gas Line Services

  • New gas line runs: For ranges, dryers, fireplaces, outdoor grills, fire pits, pool heaters
  • Appliance hookups: Connecting gas ranges, dryers, and other gas appliances
  • Gas line repair: Fixing leaks, corroded fittings, and damaged lines
  • Gas leak detection: Electronic leak detection and pressure testing
  • Gas meter and manifold work: Upgrading capacity for additional gas appliances
  • Gas line relocation: Moving lines for remodels and additions

Gas Leak Warning Signs

  • Rotten egg smell: The mercaptan odorant added to natural gas
  • Hissing sound near a gas line or appliance
  • Dead vegetation in a line above a buried gas line
  • Higher than normal gas bills without increased usage

If you smell gas, leave the house immediately and call 911 or Peoples Gas at 866-556-6002. Do not flip light switches or use electronics. Call us after the area is cleared for repair.

Typical Gas Line Costs

  • Gas appliance hookup (existing line): $100–$300
  • New gas line run (10–20 feet): $300–$800
  • New gas line run (20–50 feet): $500–$1,500
  • Outdoor gas line (grill, fire pit): $500–$2,000
  • Gas leak repair: $200–$600
  • Gas pressure test: $100–$250

All gas work requires a pressure test and permit inspection. We handle the permit process and coordinate with inspectors.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a permit for gas line work in Chicago?

Yes. All new gas line installations and major repairs require a permit and inspection. We pull the permit and schedule the inspection as part of the job.

Can you run a gas line to my outdoor grill?

Yes. We run a dedicated gas line from your meter or manifold to the outdoor cooking area with a shutoff valve. No more propane tanks to refill.

How do you test for gas leaks?

We use electronic gas detectors and perform a pressure test on the line. The line is pressurized and monitored for any pressure drop, which indicates a leak. Every new installation and repair gets a pressure test before we turn the gas on.

Can I convert from electric to gas appliances?

Yes, as long as your home has a gas supply. We run a new gas line to the appliance location and connect it. Common conversions: electric range to gas, electric dryer to gas, adding a gas fireplace.

Is it safe to have gas lines in my home?

Properly installed and maintained gas lines are very safe. Modern black iron and CSST (corrugated stainless steel tubing) piping meets strict safety codes. Regular inspection and prompt repair of any suspected leaks keeps the system safe.

sump-pump-install-replacement.content:
Sump Pump Installation & Repair

In Chicagoland, a working sump pump is the difference between a dry basement and thousands of dollars in water damage. Heavy spring rains, high water tables, and clay soil that doesn't drain well all put pressure on your foundation. We install, repair, and replace sump pumps and sump pits to keep your basement dry year-round.

Sump Pump Services

  • New sump pump installation: Including pit excavation, pump, check valve, and discharge line
  • Sump pump replacement: Swapping a failed or aging pump with a new unit
  • Battery backup installation: So your pump runs during power outages (when you need it most)
  • Sump pit installation: New pit in a basement that doesn't have one
  • Discharge line repair: Fixing frozen, clogged, or improperly routed discharge lines
  • Float switch repair: When the pump won't turn on or won't shut off

Signs Your Sump Pump Needs Attention

  • Pump runs constantly — even when it's not raining
  • Pump doesn't turn on — failed float switch or motor
  • Strange noises — grinding, rattling, or humming without pumping
  • Water in the basement despite having a pump
  • Pump is 7+ years old — approaching end of typical lifespan

Typical Sump Pump Costs

  • Sump pump replacement (standard 1/3 HP): $400–$800 installed
  • Sump pump replacement (heavy-duty 1/2 HP): $600–$1,200 installed
  • New sump pit + pump installation: $1,500–$3,500
  • Battery backup system: $500–$1,500 installed
  • Discharge line repair/reroute: $200–$600

Don't wait until the pump fails during a storm. If your pump is over 7 years old or making unusual noises, proactive replacement is much cheaper than water damage cleanup.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long do sump pumps last?

5–10 years for standard pumps. Heavy-duty cast iron pumps can last longer. Battery backups typically last 3–5 years before the battery needs replacement. We recommend replacing primary pumps at the 7-year mark as preventive maintenance.

Do I need a battery backup sump pump?

In Chicago, absolutely. Power outages during severe storms are common — exactly when your pump needs to run. A battery backup keeps pumping for 6–12 hours without power. It's one of the best investments for basement protection.

Why does my sump pump run so often?

A high water table, underground springs, or poor drainage around the foundation can cause frequent cycling. This is normal in many Chicago neighborhoods. If the pump is cycling every few minutes, consider upgrading to a higher-capacity pump.

Where should the discharge line go?

The discharge must route water away from your foundation — at least 10 feet, or to a storm drain if your municipality allows it. In Chicago, discharging to the sanitary sewer is prohibited. We make sure the discharge is properly routed and freeze-protected.

Can you install a sump pump in a basement that doesn't have a pit?

Yes. We cut a section of the concrete floor, excavate a pit, install a liner and cover, set the pump, and run the discharge line. It's a common job in older Chicago homes that were built without drainage systems.

seo.title: Sump Pump Battery Backup in Chicago, IL
seo.description: Sump pump battery backup installation in Chicago — protect your basement during power outages. Licensed plumbers. Call 833-758-6911.

sump-pump-battery-backup-install.content:
Sump Pump Battery Backup

Your sump pump is useless without power. And power outages in Chicago happen most often during severe storms — exactly when water is pouring into your sump pit the fastest. A battery backup sump pump kicks in automatically when the power goes out, keeping your basement dry when your primary pump can't run.

How Battery Backup Pumps Work

A battery backup system is a second pump installed alongside your primary sump pump, powered by a deep-cycle marine battery or a dedicated battery pack. When the power goes out (or your primary pump fails), the backup pump activates automatically. Most systems can pump 2,000–3,000 gallons on a full charge — enough for 6–12 hours of protection.

Types of Backup Systems

  • Battery-powered backup pump: Dedicated pump with a 12V deep-cycle battery. Most common and reliable option.
  • Water-powered backup pump: Uses municipal water pressure to pump — no battery needed, but uses water and requires adequate pressure.
  • Combination primary + backup: Integrated systems with built-in battery backup.

Typical Battery Backup Costs

  • Battery backup system (installed): $500–$1,500
  • Water-powered backup (installed): $400–$1,000
  • Battery replacement (every 3–5 years): $100–$250
  • Combination primary + backup system: $1,000–$2,500 installed

Compare these costs to a flooded basement — water damage restoration runs $5,000–$25,000+. A battery backup is inexpensive insurance.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long will the battery backup run during an outage?

6–12 hours of continuous pumping with a fully charged battery, depending on water volume and pump frequency. A maintenance charger keeps the battery at full charge between outages.

Do I still need a battery backup if I have a generator?

Generators are great but they don't start instantly. There's a gap between power loss and generator startup. Also, generators can fail. A battery backup provides instant, automatic protection as a secondary safety net.

How often should the battery be replaced?

Every 3–5 years for standard deep-cycle batteries. Some premium systems have longer-lasting batteries. We test the battery during annual maintenance visits and recommend replacement when capacity drops.

Can a battery backup be added to my existing sump pump?

Yes. We install the backup pump and battery alongside your existing primary pump in the same pit. No need to replace your current pump — the backup works as a companion system.

Will the battery backup alert me if it activates?

Most modern systems have an audible alarm that sounds when the backup activates, when the battery is low, or when there's a pump failure. Some premium units offer Wi-Fi alerts to your phone.

water-leak-detection-repair.content:
Water Leak Detection & Repair

A water leak you can't see can do more damage than one you can. Hidden leaks inside walls, under slabs, and in crawl spaces waste water, promote mold growth, and can cause serious structural damage before you notice. We use electronic leak detection equipment to find hidden leaks without tearing your home apart, then make the repair.

Signs of a Hidden Water Leak

  • Unexplained high water bills — a sudden spike with no change in usage
  • Water meter spinning when all fixtures are off
  • Damp or discolored spots on walls, ceilings, or floors
  • Musty smell — hidden moisture promotes mold and mildew
  • Sound of running water when nothing is on
  • Warm spot on the floor — possible hot water slab leak
  • Foundation cracks — water erosion under the slab

How We Find Leaks

  • Electronic leak detection: Acoustic sensors that amplify the sound of water escaping a pipe, pinpointing the location through walls and slabs.
  • Thermal imaging: Infrared cameras that detect temperature differences caused by moisture behind walls or under floors.
  • Pressure testing: Isolating sections of the plumbing system to narrow down the leak location.
  • Camera inspection: For drain and sewer leaks, a pipe camera shows the exact location and cause.

Typical Leak Detection and Repair Costs

  • Leak detection service: $150–$500 depending on complexity
  • Accessible pipe repair (exposed): $150–$400
  • In-wall pipe repair: $300–$800+ including wall access and patching
  • Slab leak repair (reroute): $1,500–$4,000
  • Slab leak repair (direct access): $1,000–$3,000 including concrete cutting and restoration

Early detection saves money. A small leak left unfound for months can cause thousands in water damage, mold remediation, and structural repair.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I know if I have a slab leak?

Warm or wet spots on the floor, the sound of water running under the slab, sudden increases in water bills, and cracks in the foundation are all signs. A slab leak can also cause low water pressure if it's on a supply line.

Can you find a leak without tearing up my house?

Yes. Our electronic detection equipment locates leaks through walls, floors, and concrete without destructive exploration. We only open up exactly where the leak is — no guesswork.

What causes hidden pipe leaks?

Corrosion (especially in older galvanized and copper pipes), high water pressure, freeze damage, settling foundations, and poor original installation. In Chicago, temperature extremes accelerate pipe wear.

Is a slab leak covered by insurance?

Most homeowner policies cover the resulting water damage but not the cost of finding or repairing the leak itself. Some policies also don't cover damage from gradual leaks. Check your policy and document everything.

Should I reroute the pipe or repair in place?

For slab leaks, rerouting through the ceiling or walls is often more cost-effective and less disruptive than breaking through the slab. For accessible leaks, direct repair is usually simpler and cheaper. We'll recommend the best approach for your situation.
